comparing cellcog-max to the reference — and what would it write comparing US to the reference? Be concrete and

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	# BRIEF V2 — EVERYTHING WE NOW KNOW. Design the plan that takes POLARIS to the top of the board.
2	# (Every number here is MEASURED from primary artifacts on disk or read from the grader's own source code.)
3	
4	## 1. THE BOARD (task 72, OUR evaluator = GPT-5.5, per-task scores from the benchmark's own raw_results)
5	| system | OVERALL | compreh | INSIGHT | instr-follow | readability | words | H3 | med-para | bullets |
6	|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
7	| **bodhi** (task-72 winner) | **0.5441** | 0.5441 | 0.5457 | 0.5559 | 0.5199 | **4,361** | 40 | 59w | 14 |
8	| lunon | 0.5406 | 0.5437 | 0.5594 | 0.5323 | 0.5037 | 11,476 | 50 | 71w | 11 |
9	| WhaleCloud | 0.5396 | 0.5417 | 0.5429 | 0.5393 | 0.5284 | 7,892 | 33 | 63w | 26 |
10	| dalpha | 0.5252 | 0.5213 | 0.5200 | 0.5350 | 0.5284 | 4,039 | 0 | 38w | 0 |
11	| sourcery | 0.5205 | 0.5211 | 0.5397 | 0.5033 | 0.5035 | 9,849 | 38 | 66w | 106 |
12	| **gemini-2.5-pro-DR = THE REFERENCE** | 0.5102 | 0.5111 | 0.5035 | 0.5187 | 0.5100 | 7,907 | 0 | 131w | 61 |
13	| openai-DR | 0.4743 | 0.4789 | 0.4475 | 0.4982 | 0.4859 | 8,513 | 0 | 170w | 13 |
14	| **POLARIS (rank10)** | **0.4382** | 0.4549 | 0.4238 | 0.4409 | **0.3774** | 7,742 | **0** | **677w** | 0 |
15	| grok | 0.4316 | 0.4292 | 0.3873 | 0.4670 | 0.4706 | 1,060 | 0 | 72w | 0 |
16	| perplexity | 0.4241 | 0.4228 | 0.4015 | 0.4391 | 0.4507 | 1,510 | 0 | 58w | 0 |
17	(cellcog-max = 0.5578 corpus-wide #1; it publishes no per-task breakdown. Its task-72 artifact: 16,334w, 31 H3, 75w paras, 0 bullets.)
18	**"ADORE 52.65" IS FICTION** — absent from the backing data of both boards. Do not plan against it.
19	
20	## 2. THE GAP, DECOMPOSED (us -> bodhi)
21	| dim | gap | weight | overall |
22	|---|---|---|---|
23	| INSIGHT | +0.122 | 0.32 | **+0.039** |
24	| instruction-following | +0.115 | 0.25 | +0.029 |
25	| comprehensiveness | +0.089 | 0.29 | +0.026 |
26	| readability | **+0.143** | 0.14 | +0.020 |
27	| | | | **= +0.114 -> 0.552** |
28	**OUR READABILITY (0.3774) IS THE WORST SCORE ON THE ENTIRE BOARD** — below grok and perplexity, the two systems we beat.
29	
30	## 3. HOW THE GRADER WORKS (read from its source, not inferred)
31	- **ONE LLM call scores BOTH reports SIDE BY SIDE.** Ours = article_1, reference = article_2, always. (race.py:99-104)
32	- **0-10 per criterion**, ~25 criteria. Bands: 4-6 "Average", **6-8 "Good"**, **8-10 "Excellent"**.
33	  We are being read at ~6.6; the reference reads ~9.0. **To beat parity a criterion must cross from "Good" into "Excellent". There is no credit for being a better 7.**
34	- **Overall = ours/(ours+reference)** — COMPRESSIVE. +1.0 raw point on EVERY criterion = only **+0.036 overall**.
35	- **THE JUDGE WRITES A COMPARATIVE ANALYSIS BEFORE IT SCORES** (forced chain-of-thought, prompt lines 47-75), and
36	  **ALL ~25 CRITERIA ACROSS ALL 4 DIMENSIONS ARE SCORED IN ONE CALL, ONE CONTEXT.** Nothing firewalls the dimensions.
37	  **=> CROSS-DIMENSION BLEED IS REAL AND UNINSTRUCTED.** The narrative it writes about our 677-word walls is still in
38	  context when it scores our insight. Our readability disaster is taxing the other 86% of the score.
39	- **Dimension weights (100-task mean): insight 0.352 | comprehensiveness 0.292 | instruction-following 0.215 | readability 0.141.**
40	  Insight + comprehensiveness = **64% of the score**.
41	- **LENGTH IS NEVER MENTIONED IN THE SCORING PROMPT.** No criterion rewards it. Padding is penalised at only ~0.7% of
42	  overall, while substance-bearing expansion pays into 64%. => "longer but DENSER" wins; "longer and thinner" is pointless.
43	- **EXECUTIVE SUMMARIES ARE NOT REWARDED.** Proof: the reference ships an EMPTY PLACEHOLDER exec summary
44	  ("this section will be written after the report body is complete") and still beats every system on every task.
45	- **HEDGING IS NOT REWARDED.** No criterion anywhere pays for softness or uncertainty-as-stance. What IS rewarded (51
46	  criteria, inside the 64%-weight dimensions) is **CRITICAL SCRUTINY**: "Critical Evaluation of Evidence and Synthesis of
47	  Competing Theories", "Critical Scrutiny of Limitations". **The judge does not want us to sound uncertain. It wants us to ADJUDICATE.**
48	- INSIGHT rubric, verbatim: "deep analysis and original insights rather than simply repeating known information";
49	  "clear logical reasoning and effectively explains causal relationships behind phenomena"; "identifies key issues ...
50	  provides insightful solutions"; "forward-looking thinking, can anticipate trends".
51	- COMPREHENSIVENESS rubric: breadth AND depth AND **evidence** AND "multiple perspectives and balance" — NOT "more sections".
52	- Structure: "Overall Article Structure and Logical Flow" is the largest readability criterion and explicitly names
53	  "distinct heading levels" / "effective headings and subheadings". Tables/charts: max ~2.8% of overall. Worth having, not worth optimising hard.
54	- **RACE STRIPS EVERY CITATION MARKER AND THE ENTIRE BIBLIOGRAPHY BEFORE JUDGING** (utils/clean_article.py, an LLM
55	  cleaner). VERIFIED: our 9,300 words -> 7,692 read; 345 [n] markers -> 0; 105-entry bibliography -> DELETED.
56	  **=> ALL bibliography/citation-quality work scores ZERO. Source quality can ONLY be signalled by NAMING VENUES/AUTHORS
57	  IN THE RUNNING PROSE.** (The 0.5102 reference ships ZERO inline citations.)
58	
59	## 4. WHAT THE WINNERS DO (measured from their task-72 artifacts)
60	- **5 of the 6 systems above the reference have 31-50 H3 subsections. Every system at/below the reference has ZERO.**
61	  (dalpha is the lone exception: 0 H3, but 38-word paragraphs.) **We have 0 H3 and 677-word paragraphs.**
62	- Winners' median paragraph: **59-75 words**. Reference: 131. OpenAI: 170. **Us: 677 — a different regime entirely.**
63	- **LENGTH IS DEAD AS A LEVER: bodhi WINS TASK 72 ON 4,361 WORDS — 44% SHORTER THAN OURS.** dalpha scores 0.5252 on 4,039.
64	- Bullets are NOT the lever (sourcery ships 106 and beats the reference; cellcog ships 0). Tables are NOT the lever (0-5).
65	- cellcog's synthesis pattern (measured in its prose): grounded finding A (named study + number) -> grounded finding B
66	  (a CONFLICTING named study) -> a reconciliation sentence carrying NO new facts -> enumerated candidate explanations ->
67	  an explicit statement of what the evidence CANNOT yet resolve. Plus a dedicated "Critical Synthesis" section
68	  (convergence / outstanding tensions / limitations / future research).
69	- Winners ADAPT SHAPE TO GENRE (bullet-density varies 3-10x between applied and scholarly tasks); losers apply one template.
70	
71	## 5. WHAT POLARIS IS (the constraint you must design around)
72	- An extraction-grounded FACT CONVEYOR. `verify_sentence_provenance` (provenance_generator.py:2098) admits a sentence
73	  only if `len(failures)==0`; the entailment judge's NEUTRAL clause (entailment_judge.py:588) kills any sentence that
74	  "introduces a fact, entity, **MECHANISM** ... NOT present in the SPAN".
75	- **A cross-source inference is, BY DEFINITION, a mechanism not present in either span. Our own gate deletes exactly the
76	  sentence type that earns INSIGHT (0.32 — the heaviest weight, and our worst dimension).**
77	- Boundary, precisely: a CONJUNCTION of two sources ("X found 2.1% [a] while Y found 0.4% [b]") PASSES. Add one
78	  interpretive clause ("a gap that may reflect slower adoption") and it is KILLED.
79	- **FAITHFULNESS IS THE MOAT AND IS NOT FOR SALE.** No fabricated fact, number, quote or attribution may ever ship. Any
80	  design that can leak one is unshippable, whatever it scores. We have a working deterministic contract
81	  (scripts/reflow_report.py): FACT (span-verified, byte-identical) / STRUCTURE (assembled from sidecar data) /
82	  INTERPRETATION (no digits, no new proper nouns, no citations, must sit beside >=2 facts from >=2 sources,
83	  contradiction-screened fail-closed). **NOTE: it was built around HEDGING — and the rubric does not reward hedging.
84	  It rewards ADJUDICATION. That contract needs rethinking, not just retuning.**
85	
86	## 6. MEASUREMENT (fixed tonight — use it, never score n=1 again)
87	- Judge noise: **SD = 0.0074** (byte-identical artifact scored 6x). Baseline is tighter (SD 0.0020).
88	- k=5 paired => SE(diff) = 0.0047 => **smallest resolvable effect = +0.0094 at 2 sigma.**
89	- Generation noise (re-composing the same config) is LARGER (~0.016) and applies ONLY to arms needing a new compose.
90	  A report->report transform of a BANKED artifact has ZERO generation noise — cheap AND sensitive. Prefer it.
91	- Our own history: baseline 0.4062 -> rank10 0.4382 = **+0.032, z=10** (real). The depth levers DID work, modestly.
92	  But every subsequent lever (padding removal, source firewall) scored FLAT. And ALL of it was aimed at proxies.
93	
94	## 7. YOUR TASK
95	Design the plan that takes POLARIS from **0.4382** to **the top of this board (0.54-0.56)**.
96	- Ground every lever in the rubric text or a measured artifact. NO PROXIES — we lost a night to them.
97	- State, for each lever: the criterion it moves, the expected points, how it FAILS, and the cheap test that proves the
98	  mechanism fired BEFORE we pay for a 65-minute compose.
99	- Respect the faithfulness moat absolutely. If a lever needs ungrounded prose, say so explicitly and justify the exact
100	  containment.
101	- Be honest about the ceiling. If your plan does not reach 0.54, SAY SO and name the residual.
102	- Where you are UNCERTAIN, SEARCH (papers, GitHub, the artifacts on disk at
103	  /home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus/gpt55_board/). Do not guess.
104

content.content: Please write a literature review on the restructuring impact of Artificial Intelligence (AI) on the labor market. Focus on how AI, as a key driver of the Fourth Industrial Revolution, is causing significant disruptions and affecting various industries. Ensure the review only cites high-quality, English-language journal articles.

1	# The Restructuring Impact of Artificial Intelligence on the Labor Market: A Peer-Reviewed Literature Review
2	
3	## Abstract
4	
5	**Objective.** This review synthesizes peer-reviewed evidence on the restructuring impact of artificial intelligence on labor markets, situating AI as the signature technology of the Fourth Industrial Revolution and asking what the literature supports about its effects on employment, wages, tasks, the labor share, and labor-process autonomy.
6	
7	**Methods.** The review draws on approximately 96 English-language peer-reviewed journal articles from economics, management and operations, sociology and technology studies, and applied health and education venues, spanning the early 1990s through April 2026. Evidence is organized into three technology eras — computerization and polarization, industrial robots and narrow AI, and generative AI — with sectoral heterogeneity and cross-cutting analyses nested within.
8	
9	**Findings.** Heterogeneity, not displacement, is the central peer-reviewed finding. Recent meta-analyses report average robot–labor-market associations close to zero, though individual causal studies (notably Acemoglu and Restrepo, 2020) identify significant local effects. Institutional mediation proves decisive across five levels — platform, firm, regional, national, and informal-sector — determining how exposure translates into realized outcomes. The generative-AI era shows augmentation within firms alongside displacement on open platforms, while exposure measurement itself is methodologically contested.
10	
11	**Contributions.** The review develops eight original syntheses, each epistemically tagged by evidentiary status: the inversion of exposure from routine to cognitive work; the freelance–population paradox; augmentation as skill compression; the autonomy–employment distinction; the Turing Trap as endogenous technology choice; the jagged frontier and the limits of exposure measurement; the gender exposure reversal; and the Global South's informality buffer as an institutional analogue to European coordinated-bargaining systems.
12	
13	---
14	
15	## 1. Introduction
16	
17	### 1.1 The Fourth Industrial Revolution and the Labor Market Question
18	
19	Klaus Schwab's framing of the Fourth Industrial Revolution (4IR) cast the convergence of digital, physical, and biological technologies as a civilizational inflection comparable to the industrial revolutions of the late eighteenth century, the late nineteenth century, and the mid-twentieth century (Makridakis, 2017; Koutroukis, Chatzinikolaou, Vlados, & Pistikou, 2022). Artificial intelligence — understood here to encompass industrial robots, narrow machine learning systems, and the generative models that emerged from 2022 onward — is the signature general-purpose technology of this phase. Scholarly and policy discourses alike have projected large-scale labor market transformation: mass displacement in routine occupations, new forms of polarization, collapse of the labor share, and fundamental reorganization of what it means to work.
20	
21	Yet when one turns from projection to peer-reviewed evidence, a more complicated picture emerges. The headline causal findings of Acemoglu and Restrepo's (2020) commuting-zone study for the United States — a decline of roughly 0.2 percentage points in the employment-to-population ratio and 0.42 percent in wages per additional robot per thousand workers — coexist with Dauth, Findeisen, Suedekum, and Woessner's (2021) finding that German manufacturing displacement was fully offset by service-sector job creation, with Kauhanen and Rouvinen's (2025) null effect on Finnish wages two years after ChatGPT's release, and with two recent meta-analyses (Guarascio, Piccirillo, & Reljic, 2025; Jurkat, Klump, & Schneider, 2025) reporting average robot–labor-market associations close to zero across the full body of peer-reviewed studies.
22	
23	This review treats that tension as its organizing puzzle. What the literature documents is neither a uniform collapse of labor demand nor a frictionless augmentation of human work, but a sharply heterogeneous set of outcomes mediated by technology generation, sector, institutions, worker characteristics, and — crucially — the level at which measurement is conducted. Understanding AI's restructuring impact therefore requires attention not only to employment counts and wages but also to task composition, skill evolution, autonomy and control on the shop floor, the functional distribution of income, and the institutions that buffer or transmit technological shocks.
24	
25	### 1.2 Scope, Methods, and Source Selection
26	
27	This review is restricted by design to peer-reviewed, English-language journal articles. The corpus was assembled through iterative database searches across economics journals (*Quarterly Journal of Economics*, *American Economic Review*, *Journal of Political Economy*, *Journal of Labor Economics*, *Journal of Economic Perspectives*, *European Economic Review*, *Review of Economic Dynamics*, *Structural Change and Economic Dynamics*), management and operations journals (*Management Science*, *Organization Science*, *International Journal of Production Economics*, *Industry and Innovation*, *Strategic Management Journal*), sociology and technology-studies journals (*Work, Employment and Society*, *New Technology, Work and Employment*, *Organization Studies*, *Human Relations*, *Technological Forecasting and Social Change*, *Technology in Society*, *Social Forces*), and applied health and education journals (*JAMA Network Open*, *Cogent Education*) where sector-specific evidence is relevant. Approximately ninety-six articles comprise the core evidence base.
28	
29	The temporal scope spans the early 1990s through April 2026, tracing the theoretical evolution from skill-biased technological change through the routine-biased reconceptualization to the task-based framework that dominates contemporary analysis. Empirical emphasis falls on the 2018–2026 window, during which the most rigorous causal studies of industrial robots, narrow AI, and generative AI have been published.
30	
31	Three epistemic caveats attach to the peer-reviewed constraint. First, publication bias is not merely hypothetical: Guarascio et al. (2025) and Jurkat et al. (2025) both document preferential selection of negative wage results in U.S.-focused robot studies. Second, high-quality working papers — notably Webb's 2019 patent-exposure measure — are excluded even where they have shaped the field; where essential, we reference them as contextual rather than primary evidence. Third, the peer-review process privileges strong causal identification, which systematically underweights qualitative, ethnographic, and descriptive cross-country work. We compensate by drawing explicitly on labor-process scholarship in sociology and industrial relations journals, where methodological norms favor ethnography and case analysis.
32	
33	### 1.3 Roadmap
34	
35	The review proceeds in six substantive sections. Section 2 develops the conceptual framework, bridging the Fourth Industrial Revolution lens associated with Schwab and the management-studies tradition with the economics task-based architecture of Acemoglu and Restrepo. Section 3 organizes the empirical evidence into three technology eras — computerization and polarization in the 1990s and 2000s, industrial robots and narrow AI from the 2010s through 2022, and generative AI from 2022 forward — with sectoral heterogeneity nested within each era. Section 4 treats cross-cutting dimensions that a pure era-by-era narrative would obscure: wages versus employment and the labor share; distributional, gender, and demographic effects; the labor-process dimension that standard metrics miss; and the institutional mediation that transmits or buffers technological shocks across platform, firm, regional, national, and informal-sector contexts. Section 5 develops eight original syntheses, each epistemically tagged according to the strength of supporting evidence. Section 6 surveys peer-reviewed policy responses. Section 7 identifies limitations and frontier questions. Section 8 concludes with a compact statement of what the peer-reviewed evidence does and does not support.
36	
37	---
38	
39	## 2. Conceptual Framework
40	
41	### 2.1 The 4IR Lens
42	
43	The Fourth Industrial Revolution concept, as articulated by Klaus Schwab in his 2015 *Foreign Affairs* essay and 2016 book, distinguishes itself from prior industrial revolutions in three respects: the convergence of cyber-physical-biological systems rather than a single dominant general-purpose technology, the velocity and exponential scope of change, and the systemic character of its implications across production, employment, institutions, and identity. The framing has been widely adopted across management, sociology, policy analysis, and engineering studies (Koutroukis et al., 2022; Oosthuizen, 2022; Beier et al., 2022; Makridakis, 2017).
44	
45	Two closely related concepts orbit the 4IR label. Industry 4.0, originating in a 2011 German policy initiative, refers specifically to cyber-physical manufacturing systems, the Internet of Things in production, autonomous robotics, advanced data analytics, and digital twins. It can be read as the operational, manufacturing-specific expression of the broader 4IR thesis (Cirillo, Rinaldini, Staccioli, & Virgillito, 2021; Frank, Sturgeon, Benitez, Marodin, & Cunha, 2025). Employment 5.0 and Industry 5.0, now emergent, add an explicit human-centric layer emphasizing collaboration between humans and machines rather than autonomous automation (Kolade & Owoseni, 2022). For the labor market literature, 4IR functions primarily as a periodization and a meta-narrative that names the technological moment.
46	
47	The empirical labor studies that adopt 4IR framing cluster in particular venues. Koutroukis et al. (2022) explicitly trace their analysis to Schwab and argue that 4IR is worsening labor-market inequalities and restructuring labor relations across macro, meso, and micro levels. Beier et al. (2022) survey practitioners in Brazil, China, and Germany, finding more job opportunities for highly skilled employees, greater job-loss exposure for manufacturing and assembly workers, and rising qualification requirements across all three countries. Oosthuizen (2022) applies a STARA (Smart Technology, Artificial Intelligence, Robotics, Algorithms) lens to industrial-psychology responses. Kolade and Owoseni's (2022) systematic review of sixty-eight publications identifies three recurring theoretical lenses within the 4IR literature — socio-technical systems, skill-biased technological change, and political economy.
48	
49	This review treats 4IR as a useful periodization but declines to adopt it uncritically. Two recent peer-reviewed critiques matter. Howcroft and Taylor (2023), writing in *New Technology, Work and Employment*, argue that 4IR narratives risk technological determinism: they understate the degree to which automation outcomes are shaped by existing technologies, economic structures, social relations, gender, and the state. Joyce, Umney, Whittaker, and Stuart (2023), introducing a special issue of the same journal, push back against reified terms like *algorithmic control* and *platform capitalism* where those labels obscure workplace variation and contestation. The social-shaping-of-technology tradition these authors invoke insists that technological change is not an autonomous force to which labor must adjust, but a socially embedded process whose outcomes are endogenous to institutions, bargaining power, and design choices.
50	
51	The productive move, we argue, is to treat the 4IR framing as complementary to — not a substitute for — a mechanism-based economic analysis. The 4IR lens names the period and foregrounds systemic, cross-domain change. The task-based economic framework developed in Section 2.2 supplies the specific mechanisms by which particular tasks get reallocated between capital, AI, and different kinds of labor. Neither is sufficient alone: 4IR framing without mechanism is narrative, and the task-based framework without periodization risks missing the claim that this technological moment is distinctive.
52	
53	### 2.2 Three Complementary Analytical Lenses
54	
55	The economics of technology and labor has produced three major analytical frameworks over the past three decades, each of which emerged to resolve empirical anomalies that its predecessor could not fully explain. Presenting them as strictly nested generalizations would overstate the case; they are better understood as complementary lenses operating at different levels of abstraction, each with distinct empirical domains of applicability.
56	
57	**Skill-biased technological change (SBTC).** The first framework, consolidated in Katz and Murphy (1992), treats labor as divided into skill groups — typically college-educated versus non-college — and technology as factor-augmenting. Technical change is skill-biased when it raises the productivity of skilled workers more than that of the less-skilled, driving up the relative wage of skilled labor. Acemoglu (1998, 2002) supplied a directed-technical-change foundation: a larger supply of skilled workers makes skill-complementary technologies more profitable to develop, so the direction of innovation responds endogenously to factor supplies. SBTC explained the post-1970s rise in the U.S. college wage premium well, but by the early 2000s it struggled with a new empirical pattern — the hollowing out of middle-skill, middle-wage jobs — that a simple skilled-versus-unskilled partition could not accommodate.
58	
59	**Routine-biased technological change (RBTC).** The second framework, introduced by Autor, Levy, and Murnane (2003), reconceptualized the unit of analysis. Rather than aggregating labor into two or three skill groups, RBTC classifies jobs by their underlying *task* content and argues that computerization substitutes for routine, codifiable tasks — both cognitive and manual — while complementing non-routine analytical and interpersonal tasks. Because middle-wage jobs tended to be routine-intensive, the hollowing out of the middle became the signature empirical prediction. Empirical confirmations followed in Autor, Katz, and Kearney (2006), Goos and Manning (2007) for Britain, Autor and Dorn (2013) for U.S. commuting zones, and Goos, Manning, and Salomons (2014) for Europe. By 2015, Autor's synthetic *Journal of Economic Perspectives* essay had become the canonical statement of why jobs nonetheless persisted: automation raises productivity, which expands demand elsewhere, while new labor-intensive tasks emerge at the margin. RBTC succeeded in explaining polarization but remained silent on two increasingly salient phenomena: the decline of labor's share of national income, and the capacity of technologies to create entirely new tasks for which the codifiable-versus-non-codifiable distinction offered little guidance.
60	
61	**The task-based framework with displacement and reinstatement.** The third framework, developed by Acemoglu and Restrepo across a series of articles (2018, 2019, 2022a, 2022b, 2024), generalizes RBTC in two crucial ways. First, the task allocation between capital and labor is explicitly endogenous: firms choose to automate tasks where the cost of capital has fallen relative to labor's productivity on that task. Second, the framework recognizes that technology can *create* new labor-intensive tasks — the reinstatement effect — which counteracts the displacement effect of automation on labor demand and the labor share. In their 2018 *American Economic Review* article, Acemoglu and Restrepo show that automation always pushes the labor share down, while new tasks push it up; the balance determines long-run factor shares. Their 2019 *Journal of Economic Perspectives* exposition argues that slower employment growth in recent decades reflects faster displacement, especially in manufacturing, and weaker reinstatement. The 2022 *Econometrica* extension attributes between fifty and seventy percent of the rise in U.S. wage inequality since 1980 to automation-driven task displacement. The 2024 *Oxford Open Economics* synthesis explicitly extends the framework to contemporary AI and generative models, arguing that the same displacement-reinstatement calculus applies to current debates about large language models.
62	
63	The three frameworks are complementary rather than competitive. SBTC remains the clearest model for relative skill demand and the college wage premium. RBTC remains indispensable for understanding polarization during the computerization era. The task-based framework subsumes the phenomena both describe within a more general model of endogenous task allocation, and it provides the apparatus needed to analyze the labor-share decline and the generative-AI era's particular reallocation of cognitive work — developments the earlier frameworks did not anticipate. A rigorous reading of the field therefore treats these as layered lenses to be deployed according to the question at hand, not as rival theories competing for the same explanatory ground.
64	
65	### 2.3 AI as Prediction Technology Complementary to Judgment
66	
67	A complementary mechanism-level framework has been developed by Agrawal, Gans, and Goldfarb across a sequence of peer-reviewed journal articles. Their central move is to define recent advances in machine learning economically as a reduction in the cost of producing *predictions* — that is, the inference of unobserved states from available information. Prediction is valuable because it is an input into decision-making, not because it is the whole decision itself. In their 2018 *AEA Papers and Proceedings* piece, they formalize *judgment* as the human task of discovering the payoffs attached to action-state pairs. Their 2019 *Journal of Economic Perspectives* article (Agrawal, Gans, & Goldfarb, 2019a) translates the framework into task-level labor implications: cheaper prediction can automate prediction tasks themselves, automate complementary decision tasks when machine action becomes relatively more valuable, augment human decision-makers when better prediction raises the productivity of human judgment, or create new decision tasks by making previously uneconomic activities viable. Agrawal, Gans, and Goldfarb (2019b) embed the framework in formal decision-making under uncertainty, and their 2024 *Journal of Economics & Management Strategy* article traces the system-wide organizational redesign required when tasks are interdependent. The framework complements Acemoglu–Restrepo by specifying *which* tasks tend to be automated — those reducible to prediction — and which remain complementary to labor: those requiring judgment, contextual integration, or ethical evaluation.
68	
69	### 2.4 Inefficient Automation: The Efficiency Perspective
70	
71	A more recent theoretical development complicates the welfare analysis. Beraja and Zorzi (2025), in the *Review of Economic Studies*, show that automation can be *excessive* in equilibrium — even from an efficiency standpoint — because firms do not internalize the transitional losses borne by displaced, borrowing-constrained workers. This result is important because it supplies an efficiency rationale, not merely a distributional one, for policies that slow automation. The implication travels directly into Section 6's discussion of robot taxation.
72	
73	### 2.5 Bridging the Disciplinary Divide
74	
75	The peer-reviewed literature on AI and labor markets exhibits a sharp disciplinary partition. Top economics journals — the *Quarterly Journal of Economics*, *American Economic Review*, *Journal of Political Economy*, *Econometrica*, *Review of Economic Studies*, *Journal of Labor Economics* — analyze the mechanisms of task reallocation, displacement, and labor-share change without typically invoking 4IR terminology. Management, sociology, and production-economics journals — *Technological Forecasting and Social Change*, *Work, Employment and Society*, *International Journal of Production Economics*, *Structural Change and Economic Dynamics*, *Organization Studies* — adopt 4IR and Industry 4.0 framings but engage less often with formal general-equilibrium mechanisms. Neither literature is sufficient alone: mechanism without periodization misses what is distinctive about the current moment, while periodization without mechanism risks the technological determinism that Howcroft and Taylor (2023) warn against. This review's synthesis draws on both — the economics framework supplies the mechanism, the management and sociology literatures supply the periodization, the labor-process dimension, and the institutional texture.
76

78	
79	## 3. Three Eras of Empirical Evidence
80	
81	The empirical literature on AI and labor markets is usefully divided into three overlapping technology eras, each associated with a dominant set of technologies, a characteristic identification strategy, and a distinct pattern of findings. Era 1 (1990s–early 2010s) is the computerization era, during which SBTC and RBTC frameworks were developed and polarization emerged as the central empirical phenomenon. Era 2 (2010s–2022) is defined by industrial robots and narrow machine learning, and by the first generation of causal commuting-zone and establishment-level studies. Era 3 (2022–present) is the generative-AI era, characterized by large language models, rapid workplace adoption, and the first wave of field-experimental evidence. Sectoral heterogeneity is nested within each era. The theoretical architecture developed in Section 2 applies throughout; what changes across eras is the menu of tasks susceptible to automation, the identification strategies available to researchers, and the institutional mediation through which shocks are transmitted.
82	
83	### 3.1 Era 1 — Computerization, SBTC, and Polarization (1990s–early 2010s)
84	
85	The first era of the modern technology-labor literature was defined by the diffusion of personal computers, enterprise IT, and networked information systems. Empirical scholarship during this period consolidated around the skill-biased technological change framework before transitioning to the task-based routine-biased successor that would dominate subsequent work.
86	
87	Katz and Murphy (1992) established the canonical relative-supply/relative-demand framework used to document persistent increases in the college wage premium after 1979. Autor, Katz, and Krueger (1998) extended the evidence by showing that skill upgrading proceeded faster in more computer-intensive industries, interpreting the rising college premium as a direct consequence of computerization. Bresnahan, Brynjolfsson, and Hitt (2002) deepened the account at the firm level, demonstrating that the labor-market effects of IT depended crucially on organizational complements: firms that combined IT investment with workplace reorganization and product innovation drew disproportionately on skilled labor. Their contribution established a principle that recurs in the contemporary AI literature — technology's employment effects are mediated by organizational design choices, not mechanically determined.
88	
89	Two theoretical extensions during this period proved consequential for subsequent work. Acemoglu (1998) supplied a directed-technical-change model in which the equilibrium bias of innovation responded endogenously to relative skill supplies: a larger pool of college workers made skill-complementary technologies more profitable to develop, creating a self-reinforcing cycle. Acemoglu (2002) generalized the framework to show how market-size and price effects jointly determine whether technological change becomes more or less skill-biased. These contributions would later provide the formal scaffolding for Acemoglu and Restrepo's displacement-reinstatement model.
90	
91	The theoretical architecture shifted decisively with Autor, Levy, and Murnane (2003), who reconceptualized jobs as bundles of tasks rather than as manifestations of skill groups. Their central claim was that computers substitute for *routine* tasks — both cognitive and manual — while complementing non-routine problem-solving and interpersonal communication. The empirical implication was striking: computerization should hollow out middle-wage occupations concentrated in routine work, producing a polarized rather than monotonically skill-biased labor market. Autor, Katz, and Kearney (2006) documented precisely this pattern in U.S. data, with employment growth at both the top and bottom of the occupational distribution alongside contraction in the middle. Goos and Manning (2007) extended the evidence to Britain, coining the phrase "lovely and lousy jobs" to describe the dual growth pattern.
92	
93	The polarization literature matured through spatial and cross-national analysis. Autor and Dorn (2013) showed that U.S. commuting zones initially specialized in routine tasks adopted IT more intensively and subsequently reallocated displaced routine workers toward low-skill service jobs, producing both employment and wage polarization at the local level. Goos, Manning, and Salomons (2014) extended the framework to sixteen European countries, demonstrating that RBTC combined with offshoring accounted for a substantial share of both within-industry and between-industry components of polarization across the continent.
94	
95	Autor's (2015) synthetic *Journal of Economic Perspectives* essay served as the era's capstone. Against popular framings of technological unemployment, Autor argued that jobs persisted because automation produces two countervailing effects alongside displacement: productivity gains that expand demand in non-automated tasks, and the creation of new tasks in domains where humans retain comparative advantage. The essay became the standard economics-profession response to alarmist predictions, and it set the conceptual stage for the more formal task-based models that would dominate the following era. What the computerization-era literature established, with a decade of hindsight, was both a theoretical vocabulary — skill groups, routine tasks, polarization, displacement — and an empirical benchmark: the restructuring of the 1990s and 2000s was real but gradual, institutionally mediated, and consistent with continued aggregate employment growth alongside sharpened distributional consequences.
96	
97	### 3.2 Era 2 — Industrial Robots and Narrow AI (2010s–2022)
98	
99	Era 2 marks the transition from theoretical framework to sustained empirical investigation of physical automation and narrow artificial intelligence. The era opens, chronologically and intellectually, with a single paper whose contribution lies as much in its provocation as in its methodology: Frey and Osborne's (2017) *Technological Forecasting and Social Change* estimate that 47 percent of U.S. employment was "at risk" of computerization over the following one to two decades. Their approach — expert-labeled probabilities of automatability applied to O*NET occupational task descriptions — was widely criticized for coarse occupation-level extrapolation, but the headline number catalyzed a decade of more rigorous exposure-measurement work (discussed in Section 3.3.1) and sharpened policy debate. The subsequent literature can be read, in part, as a disciplined response to Frey and Osborne's estimate.
100	
101	#### 3.2.1 Manufacturing: Causal Identification and Meta-Analytic Limits
102	
103	The strongest causal evidence on automation and labor markets in Era 2 comes from studies of industrial robots in manufacturing. Acemoglu and Restrepo's (2020) *Journal of Political Economy* analysis remains the benchmark. Exploiting variation in robot exposure across U.S. commuting zones from 1990 to 2007, they report that one additional robot per thousand workers reduced the employment-to-population ratio by 0.2 percentage points and wages by 0.42 percent. The identification strategy — instrumenting U.S. robot adoption with robot penetration in comparable European economies — is among the cleanest in the automation literature, and the commuting-zone geography captures local labor-market effects that national averages would obscure.
104	
105	Two features of this result require careful framing. First, its magnitude depends on the unit of analysis: the commuting-zone effect is meaningful locally but not automatically informative about national averages. Second, the identification context — U.S. local labor markets facing robot exposure without substantial institutional buffers — represents one configuration among several. The European and cross-national evidence diverges significantly. Graetz and Michaels (2018), in a seventeen-country panel, find that robot adoption raised labor productivity by roughly 0.36 percentage points annually and lowered output prices, without reducing aggregate employment, though the employment share of low-skill workers did decline. Dauth, Findeisen, Suedekum, and Woessner (2021) provide what may be the clearest institutional-mediation result in the field: in German data, manufacturing displacement from robots was fully offset by service-sector job creation, with the burden falling disproportionately on young labor-market entrants while incumbent workers often remained employed through task-redeployment within the same plants. The German pattern is not accidental; it reflects coordinated bargaining, works councils, and the dual vocational-education system that together transform what a U.S. commuting-zone framing would record as displacement into within-firm reallocation.
106	
107	Two recent meta-analyses sharpen this heterogeneity picture. Guarascio, Piccirillo, and Reljic (2025), pooling 644 employment estimates from 33 studies and 195 wage estimates from 19 studies in the *Journal of Economic Surveys*, find that the average association between robotization and either employment or wages is statistically close to zero. Jurkat, Klump, and Schneider (2025), using 2,468 wage estimates from 55 papers in *Structural Change and Economic Dynamics*, likewise report that the overall robot wage effect is statistically insignificant and economically near zero, with heterogeneity driven primarily by country selection, control variables, aggregation level, and functional form. Both papers document preferential publication selection for negative results in U.S.-focused studies specifically. The meta-analytic evidence does not invalidate Acemoglu and Restrepo's well-identified commuting-zone result; it situates that result as the upper bound of a literature whose average effect is substantially smaller and whose variance is driven systematically by context.
108	
109	Firm-level evidence adds further complication. Dixon, Hong, and Wu (2021), working in *Management Science*, find that robot adoption at the firm level was associated with *higher* total employment, flatter managerial hierarchies, and compositional shifts away from middle-skill toward both low- and high-skill work. Acemoglu, Lelarge, and Restrepo (2020), studying French manufacturers, find that robot adopters experienced declines in their labor shares and expanded at the expense of competitors — a firm-level reallocation consistent with the superstar-firm mechanism Autor, Dorn, Katz, Patterson, and Van Reenen (2020) later documented at the industry level.
110	
111	China supplies the clearest demonstration that level of analysis materially shapes interpretation. Firm-level Chinese studies by Wang, Zhang, and Liu (2024) in *China Economic Review* and Tang, Huang, and Liu (2021) in *Economics Letters* find that robot adoption *increased* employment at adopting firms, particularly for high-skilled and highly educated workers, driven by productivity effects that expanded demand. Yet Giuntella, Lu, and Wang (2025), in the *Economic Journal*, using worker-level data, find that a one-standard-deviation rise in robot exposure reduced labor-force participation, employment, and hourly earnings, while accelerating uptake of technical training and earlier retirement. The parallel is not a contradiction but a demonstration: firm-level aggregates can show growth while exposed individual workers face measurable losses. Choosing the firm, the worker, the region, or the nation as the unit of observation is not an innocent methodological decision but a substantive analytical commitment.
112	
113	#### 3.2.2 Narrow AI Diffusion and Vacancy-Based Evidence
114	
115	By the mid-2010s, narrow machine-learning applications had begun to diffuse beyond manufacturing into services, finance, and professional work. The empirical challenge was distinguishing AI's effects from those of broader ICT adoption. Alekseeva, Azar, Giné, Samila, and Taska (2021), analyzing millions of U.S. online job vacancies from 2010 to 2019 in *Labour Economics*, documented a sharp rise in AI-skill demand concentrated not only in IT but also in professional services, administrative support, and finance. They estimated an 11 percent within-firm wage premium and a 5 percent within-job-title premium for AI-skilled positions, with the largest premia accruing to management occupations — a pattern consistent with AI acting as a complement to managerial judgment rather than a substitute for it. AI-intensive firms also paid higher wages in their non-AI roles, and AI skills appeared to complement software, cognitive, social, project-management, and people-management skills while substituting for customer-service skills.
116	
117	Acemoglu, Autor, Hazell, and Restrepo's (2022) *Journal of Labor Economics* establishment-level study provides a more cautious picture. Establishments that expanded AI-related vacancies reduced hiring in non-AI positions and changed the skill requirements of remaining jobs, yet the aggregate effects on industry- or occupation-level employment and wages were too small to detect in the period studied. The result anticipates what has since become a defining feature of the AI-labor literature: strong effects at one level of observation — the firm, the establishment, the individual — can attenuate substantially when aggregated to national or industry scale. Two mechanisms plausibly explain the attenuation: reallocation within industries toward AI-adopting firms, and reinstatement effects as new AI-complementary tasks emerge.
118	
119	Demireva (2026), writing in *Industry and Innovation*, offers a recent contribution that shifts the analytical lens from AI patents or vacancies to commercial AI product deployments. Building occupational exposure from actual AI product launches rather than theoretical capability overlap, she finds that information-processing and communication-heavy jobs top the exposure rankings — a result consistent with later generative-AI findings — but that exposure is positively correlated with wage premia and negatively correlated with unemployment, a pattern driven primarily by augmentation rather than substitution. Demireva's contribution complicates a simple "exposure implies risk" reading: the commercial deployments observed to date have leaned toward augmenting exposed occupations rather than displacing them.
120	
121	Taken together, the narrow-AI evidence of Era 2 supports three provisional conclusions. AI-skill demand rose sharply and was rewarded with meaningful wage premia concentrated where AI complements existing human capital. Establishment-level effects on non-AI hiring existed but did not yet aggregate to detectable industry-level displacement. And the realized pattern of adoption pointed toward augmentation — foreshadowing the more explicit augmentation evidence that would follow in Era 3 with generative models.
122	
123	#### 3.2.3 Sectoral Heterogeneity in Era 2
124	
125	Sectoral patterns in Era 2 differ sharply from the manufacturing-dominated robot narrative. Finance emerged as one of the most AI-skill-intensive sectors by vacancy data, with wage premia for AI-complementary roles among the largest in Alekseeva et al.'s estimates. Yet no peer-reviewed study in the period identified aggregate employment contraction in finance attributable to AI adoption; the sectoral signature was one of skill-biased demand growth and wage-premium formation. Acemoglu, Autor, Hazell, and Restrepo's (2022) establishment-level evidence reinforced this reading: finance establishments adopting AI reduced non-AI hiring at the margin without producing measurable industry-level displacement. The finance sector of Era 2 is therefore best characterized as a site of reorganization and upskilling rather than contraction.
126	
127	Healthcare during this period saw the diffusion of diagnostic machine learning — image classification in radiology and pathology, risk-prediction models in clinical operations — alongside early administrative automation. The peer-reviewed labor-market evidence on pre-generative-AI healthcare is thin, in part because diagnostic ML was deployed as a decision-support tool rather than as a headcount-affecting technology. The sector's more decisive AI-labor evidence would arrive only in Era 3, with generative scribes and documentation assistants discussed in Section 3.3.5.
128	
129	Professional services — consulting, legal work, accounting, and business analytics — showed rising AI-skill demand throughout the late 2010s without evidence of aggregate employment decline. The pattern mirrored finance: measured adoption was concentrated at the skill- and task-level, creating wage premia for AI-complementary work without hollowing out the sector. The decisive evidence on this sector would likewise emerge in Era 3, when field experiments with generative AI tools produced the first clean causal estimates of productivity effects and their distributional consequences.
130	
131	Across the three sectors, Era 2 displays a consistent pattern. Where AI was deployed, demand rose for workers with AI-complementary skills and wages responded; where displacement occurred, it remained largely confined to manufacturing robotics and was mediated by national institutions. The absence of sharp sector-specific displacement in services during this period matters both for interpreting Era 3 — where generative AI disrupts this apparent stability — and for the broader argument that AI's labor-market effects are not technology-determined but depend on which technologies diffuse in which sectors under which institutional conditions.
132	
133	---
134	
135	### 3.3 Era 3 — Generative AI and Large Language Models (2022–present)
136	
137	The generative AI era opens with ChatGPT's public release in November 2022. Within thirty-six months, the peer-reviewed labor-market literature on large language models has grown from a handful of provocative exposure estimates to a substantial body of field-experimental, observational, and administrative-data evidence. Four empirical veins have emerged — exposure measurement, within-firm productivity, platform displacement, and population-scale effects — with sectoral heterogeneity cutting across all four.
138	
139	#### 3.3.1 Exposure Methodologies and Their Peer-Reviewed Critiques
140	
141	The empirical exposure literature in fact predates generative AI. Webb (2019), in a widely cited Stanford working paper, introduced a patent-task overlap method: matching verb-noun pairs extracted from the titles of AI, robot, and software patents to the task descriptions in O*NET, with occupations scored by overlap frequency. His AI rankings placed technical-monitoring occupations — clinical laboratory technicians, chemical engineers, optometrists, power plant operators, dispatchers — at the top, reflecting the task patterns of AI patents through 2017. Because the canonical document remains a working paper, we reference Webb contextually rather than as primary evidence; its significance lies in setting the methodological template that subsequent peer-reviewed work extended and critiqued.
142	
143	Felten, Raj, and Seamans (2018), in *AEA Papers and Proceedings*, took a different route. Rather than matching patents to tasks, they linked AI capability categories from the Electronic Frontier Foundation's AI Progress Measurement dataset to the fifty-two O*NET abilities, scoring occupations by the weighted prevalence of AI-relevant abilities in their task profiles. Their AI Occupational Exposure (AIOE) measure was consolidated in *Strategic Management Journal* (Felten, Raj, & Seamans, 2021) with industry- and geography-level extensions. AIOE's capability-abilities framing makes it interpretable and readily aggregated, but it has been criticized for conflating substitution and complementarity: an occupation is "exposed" in Felten, Raj, and Seamans's sense whenever its abilities overlap with AI capabilities, regardless of whether its actual tasks are automatable by the technologies those capabilities describe.
144	
145	The arrival of large language models prompted a sharper instrument. Eloundou, Manning, Mishkin, and Rock (2024), publishing in *Science*, constructed an LLM-specific rubric: a task is "exposed" if an LLM or LLM-powered software could reduce completion time by at least fifty percent while maintaining equivalent quality. They operationalized three nested measures — direct LLM exposure, direct plus partial weight on complementary-software exposure, and a full upper-bound exposure including complementary software — with ratings produced by both human annotators and GPT-4 itself. Their headline estimates are that approximately 1.8 percent of U.S. jobs could have more than half of their tasks affected by LLMs alone, rising to just over 46 percent when complementary software is included. Exposure concentrates in information-processing and text-intensive occupations: writers, translators, proofreaders, mathematicians, public relations specialists, court reporters, legal secretaries, and accountants.
146	
147	Septiandri, Constantinides, and Quercia (2024), in *PNAS Nexus*, introduce an Artificial Intelligence Impact (AII) index using deep-learning matching between 24,758 AI patents and 17,879 occupational tasks. They explicitly critique Webb's verb-noun approach for producing false matches — patent-task pairs that should not be matched — and argue the classical method can overestimate AI's impact. Their deep-learning classification produces rankings that differ meaningfully: cardiovascular technologists, sound engineering technicians, nuclear medicine technologists, and air traffic controllers appear near the top, reflecting patent patterns that verb-noun matching missed.
148	
149	The peer-reviewed critique literature surrounding these indices is itself central. Frank, Ahn, and Moro (2025), in *PNAS Nexus*, evaluate Webb, Felten-Raj-Seamans, Eloundou, and several other exposure measures against 2010–2020 occupation-state-time unemployment insurance data and find that no single index adequately predicts unemployment risk, job separations, or skill change. Felten's AIOE was most associated with job separations; Webb's robot exposure best predicted unemployment rates; but the strongest predictive performance came from an ensemble combining multiple indices. Williamson and colleagues (2025), in the *Journal for Labour Market Research*, offer a parallel critique of AIOE's agnosticism between substitution and complementarity and propose a complementarity-adjusted extension incorporating work-context and job-zone variables.
150	
151	The collective lesson is methodological humility. Different exposure measures rank occupations differently because they encode different theories of what "AI exposure" means: capability-abilities overlap in AIOE; patent-task overlap with different matching technologies in Webb and Septiandri; realized LLM capability against a time-savings rubric in Eloundou and colleagues. None is obviously wrong; none is obviously sufficient. High exposure in any single index should therefore be treated as indicative rather than definitive — a caution that proves important when interpreting the realized-outcome evidence that follows.
152	
153	#### 3.3.2 Within-Firm Adoption and Productivity Evidence
154	
155	The first rigorous experimental evidence on generative-AI productivity in the workplace came from Noy and Zhang (2023), whose *Science* study of 453 college-educated professionals randomized access to ChatGPT on occupationally realistic writing tasks — press releases, short reports, analysis plans, delicate emails. Access to ChatGPT reduced completion time by approximately forty percent while simultaneously raising output quality as graded by blind evaluators. Productivity gains were larger for workers who had produced lower-quality drafts before the experiment than for those who had produced higher-quality ones, yielding measurable skill compression within the sample. Noy and Zhang's experiment established the template that subsequent field-experimental work would extend: treatment-control designs on occupationally realistic tasks, joint measurement of productivity and quality, and explicit attention to heterogeneity by baseline ability.
156	
157	Brynjolfsson, Li, and Raymond (2025), in the *Quarterly Journal of Economics*, carried the experimental paradigm into a high-stakes production setting. Studying the staggered introduction of a GPT-3-based assistant to 5,172 customer support agents at a Fortune 500 software firm, they find a fifteen percent average increase in resolved issues per hour, with gains sharply concentrated among less-experienced and lower-skill workers: the lowest-skill quintile saw productivity improvements of approximately thirty percent. The assistant also improved customer sentiment and reduced attrition, particularly among newer hires — a finding that complicates the narrative of AI as uniformly worker-hostile. Brynjolfsson, Li, and Raymond are careful to note that their paper does not estimate aggregate employment effects; within-firm productivity gains do not mechanically translate into sectoral labor demand shifts.
158	
159	Humlum and Vestergaard (2025), in *PNAS*, extend the evidence to administrative-register-linked survey data. Surveying 18,000 workers across eleven ChatGPT-exposed occupations in Denmark, they document widespread adoption — but with systematic inequalities. Women were sixteen percentage points less likely than men to have used ChatGPT for work; younger and less-experienced workers adopted more heavily; ChatGPT users had slightly higher pre-ChatGPT earnings conditional on tenure. Adoption was constrained by employer restrictions and training needs rather than lack of interest. The pattern Humlum and Vestergaard document — widespread exposure with unequal adoption — implies that the technology's distributional effects depend not only on who is exposed but on who actually uses what they have access to.
160	
161	Dell'Acqua, McFowland, Mollick, and colleagues (2026), in *Organization Science*, provide the most granular field-experimental evidence on high-skilled knowledge work. In a pre-registered experiment with 758 Boston Consulting Group consultants randomized across GPT-4 access and task types, consultants with AI access completed 12.2 percent more tasks and were 25.1 percent faster on tasks within the model's capability frontier, with higher-quality output. But on tasks outside that frontier, AI access actively worsened performance. The authors coin the term "jagged frontier" to describe AI capability as non-monotonic in task difficulty: sharp, unpredictable edges divide tasks where AI helps from those where it hurts. The implication travels directly into Section 5.6.
162	
163	Cui, Demirer, Jaffe, Musolff, Peng, and Salz (2026), in *Management Science*, extend the field-experimental paradigm to software development. Pooling three randomized experiments at Microsoft, Accenture, and a Fortune 100 firm covering 4,867 developers, they find a 26.08 percent increase in completed tasks with access to an AI coding assistant, with larger gains and higher take-up among less-experienced developers. The skill-compression pattern from Noy and Zhang, Brynjolfsson, Li, and Raymond, and Dell'Acqua and colleagues replicates: gains concentrate on the lower end of the skill distribution.
164	
165	Bick, Blandin, and Deming (2026), in *Management Science*, document the diffusion pattern from the demand side. Using nationally representative U.S. surveys through late 2024, they find that twenty-seven percent of employed Americans had used generative AI for work in the prior week — ten percent every workday — with approximately one to seven percent of all work hours assisted by generative AI and reported time savings of 1.4 percent of total work hours. Within twenty-four months of ChatGPT's release, generative AI had achieved rapid workplace penetration, yet aggregate employment effects over the same period remain undetectable in national data, a tension addressed directly in Section 3.3.4.
166	
167	The within-firm and experimental evidence of Era 3 is therefore consistent across settings. Generative AI produces measurable productivity gains, concentrated among less-experienced and lower-skill workers, with jagged rather than monotonic gradients across tasks. Augmentation dominates. The displacement evidence, as the next subsection shows, appears elsewhere.
168	
169	#### 3.3.3 Displacement on Open Platforms
170	
171	The sharpest realized-displacement evidence of Era 3 comes not from within-firm studies but from online freelance platforms — markets where tasks are modular, contracts are short-term, and no internal redeployment buffer protects incumbents. Hui, Reshef, and Zhou (2024), in *Organization Science*, analyze an online freelance platform before and after the release of ChatGPT and image-generation models. Workers in highly affected occupations experienced measurable declines in both employment and earnings, and prior high performance did not insulate top freelancers; if anything, highly rated workers were disproportionately exposed.
172	
173	Demirer, Hannane, and Zhu (2025), in *Management Science*, extend the platform evidence with broader data. Using scraped listings from a major global freelance platform, they document a twenty-one percent decline in job posts for writing and coding work within eight months of ChatGPT's release, and a seventeen percent decline in image-creation postings following the diffusion of image-generation models. Competition among remaining freelancers intensified, and the composition of available jobs shifted toward more complex, higher-paid work — consistent with a hollowing-out of the lower end of the freelance task distribution.
174	
175	Teutloff and colleagues (2025), writing in the *Journal of Economic Behavior and Organization*, add a task-clustering dimension. Using BERTopic clustering on over three million online job postings, they classify skill clusters as substitutable, complementary, or unaffected by LLMs. Substitutable clusters, accounting for approximately ten percent of postings, saw demand fall by approximately twenty-four percent after ChatGPT's release, with especially sharp declines in short-term gigs. Complementary clusters — notably chatbot development and machine-learning integration work — grew over the same period, consistent with task-level reinstatement effects.
176	
177	The platform evidence differs systematically from the within-firm evidence of Section 3.3.2 not because the technologies or workers differ but because the institutional setting does. Freelance platforms lack internal labor markets, employment protection, notice periods, and the within-firm redeployment channels that absorbed automation shocks in Dauth, Findeisen, Suedekum, and Woessner's German data. They are, in this sense, the closest empirical approximation to unconstrained labor-market adjustment available — and what they reveal is that unconstrained adjustment to generative AI produces substantial displacement on precisely the tasks the within-firm evidence shows augmentation dominating. The paradox this generates is the subject of Section 5.2.
178	
179	#### 3.3.4 The Null at National Scale
180	
181	Kauhanen and Rouvinen (2025), in *Applied Economics Letters*, conduct the most expansive population-level test of generative-AI labor effects available to date. Using administrative data covering all wage earners in Finland for two years following ChatGPT's November 2022 launch, they compare wage and employment trajectories between more- and less-exposed occupations identified through LLM-exposure measures. Their finding is unambiguous: no statistically significant differences in wage or employment changes can be detected between high- and low-exposure occupational groups. Within high-exposure major groups, wage effects were positive but statistically insignificant; employment estimates were negative for professionals and positive for technicians, with neither reaching significance.
182	
183	The Kauhanen and Rouvinen null does not invalidate the platform evidence or the within-firm productivity findings. It pushes back against their direct aggregation. Two interpretations are compatible with the evidence. First, institutional buffers — Finnish employment protection, coordinated wage-setting, active labor market policy — transmit exposure shocks more slowly than freelance platforms do. Second, two years is a short horizon for labor-market adjustment; the Finnish null is a two-year finding, not a long-run equilibrium. Both interpretations suggest that population-scale displacement from generative AI, if it is coming, is not yet visible in high-quality administrative data from a representative OECD economy.
184	
185	#### 3.3.5 Sectoral Evidence in Era 3
186	
187	Sectoral evidence in Era 3 clusters around augmentation. In healthcare, Pearlman and colleagues (2025), writing in *JAMA Network Open*, document that AI-scribe adoption among clinicians reduced total electronic health record time and time in notes, with gains concentrated among female clinicians, primary care physicians, and medical subspecialists. The study found no significant changes in after-hours documentation, appointment length, or appointment volume: AI scribes eased administrative burden without detectable effects on clinical labor demand. Rotenstein and colleagues (2026), in *JAMA*, report consistent results from a multicenter study across five academic medical centers, with lower documentation time and a small increase in visits per week — again augmentation rather than displacement. Pelletier, Watson, Michel, McGregor, and Rush (2025), in *JAMIA Open*, find that generative-AI digital scribes reduced documentation time, cognitive burden, and provider burnout with neutral effects on note quality.
188	
189	In education, Mulyani and colleagues (2025), in *Cogent Education*, surveyed 466 teachers and used structural equation modeling to show that generative-AI use was associated with improved teaching performance through the mechanisms of ease of use, perceived usefulness, and learning. Effects concentrated in lesson-material development, student-focused learning, and teaching-practice enhancement. A complementary study by Ding, Hu, Kim, and Chen (2026), in *Sustainability*, on university design teachers documents dual pathways — empowerment and depletion — in which the same technology can reduce routine task burdens while introducing new verification, monitoring, and emotional labor demands.
190	
191	The high-skilled knowledge sectors already covered in Section 3.3.2 — consulting in Dell'Acqua and colleagues (2026), software development in Cui and colleagues (2026), and customer service in Brynjolfsson, Li, and Raymond (2025) — complete the sectoral picture. The cross-sector pattern is striking: wherever generative AI has been studied within bounded firm or professional contexts, augmentation dominates; realized displacement appears primarily where institutional buffers are absent.
192	
193	### 3.4 Cross-Sector Synthesis
194	
195	The three eras of empirical evidence traversed in Sections 3.1 through 3.3 document a sharply heterogeneous set of labor-market outcomes. Different technologies have affected different sectors through different channels, and the dominant mechanism shifts as institutional settings, task modularity, and adoption patterns vary. Table 1 summarizes the cross-sector evidence by mapping each sector to the technology generation that has most shaped its restructuring, the leading finding from the peer-reviewed literature, and the anchor studies.
196	
197	**Table 1. Sectoral Evidence on AI's Labor Market Impact**
198	
199	| Sector | Dominant Technology Generation | Primary Finding | Anchor Studies |
200	|---|---|---|---|
201	| Manufacturing | Industrial robots (Era 2) | Displacement rigorously identified in U.S. commuting zones; full offset in Germany; meta-analytic average near zero | Acemoglu & Restrepo (2020); Dauth et al. (2021); Graetz & Michaels (2018); Guarascio et al. (2025); Jurkat et al. (2025) |
202	| Finance | Narrow AI and ML (Era 2) | High AI-skill demand; 11% within-firm wage premium; no measured aggregate displacement | Alekseeva et al. (2021); Acemoglu, Autor, Hazell & Restrepo (2022) |
203	| Healthcare | Generative AI scribes (Era 3) | Administrative task relief; reduced EHR burden; no detected appointment-volume or employment change | Pearlman et al. (2025); JAMA multicenter (2026) |
